### SETCONSOLE

**Action**

Changes the display state of the command-prompt window in which KiXtart is running.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETCONSOLE("mode")
```

**Parameters**

- **Mode**: String that specifies the new display state. The following table shows the display states that are supported by this function.

<table><thead><tr><th><p><strong>SHOW </strong></p></th><th><p>Show window</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>HIDE </strong></p></td><td><p>Hide window</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>FOREGROUND </strong></p></td><td><p>Move window to foreground</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>ALWAYSONTOP </strong></p></td><td><p>Bring window to top</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MINIMIZE </strong></p></td><td><p>Minimize window</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MAXIMIZE </strong></p></td><td><p>Maximize window</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Remarks**

If a window is hidden, it does not disappear from the system, but remains active.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Display state changed</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
If SetConsole ("FOREGROUND") = 0
   ? "Console moved to foreground......"
Endif
```

---

### SETDEFAULTPRINTER

**Action**

Sets the default printer to which applications send print jobs.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETDEFAULTPRINTER ("printer name")
```

**Parameters**

- **Printer name**: String that specifies the fully qualified name of the printer to set as the default printer. Note that if the printer involved was connected to using AddPrinterConnection, you must include both the servername and the printer name.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Default printer set</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
If AddPrinterConnection ("\\vleerbeer\hp laserjet 4") = 0
   ? "Added printer connection...."
   If SetDefaultPrinter ("\\vleerbeer\hp laserjet 4") = 0
      ? "Set default printer to HP LaserJet 4...."
   Endif
Endif
```

---

### SETFILEATTR

**Action**

Sets the attributes of a file.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETFILEATTR ("file name", attributes)
```

**Parameter**

- **File name**: Identifies the file of which you want to set the attributes.

- **Attributes**: Attributes to set for the file. The attributes can be one or more of the following values:

<table><thead><tr><th><p>1</p></th><th><p>Read only</p></th><th><p>The file or directory is read-only. Applications can read the file but cannot write to it or delete it. In the case of a directory, applications cannot delete it.</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>2</p></td><td><p>Hidden</p></td><td><p>The file or directory is hidden. It is not included in an ordinary directory listing.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>4</p></td><td><p>System</p></td><td><p>The file or directory is part of, or is used exclusively by, the operating system.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>32</p></td><td><p>Archive</p></td><td><p>The file or directory is an archive file or directory. Applications use this attribute to mark files for backup or removal.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>128</p></td><td><p>Normal</p></td><td><p>The file or directory has no other attributes set. This attribute is valid only if used alone.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>256</p></td><td><p>Temporary</p></td><td><p>The file is being used for temporary storage. File systems attempt to keep all of the data in memory for quicker access rather than flushing the data back to mass storage. A temporary file should be deleted by the application as soon as it is no longer needed.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>4096</p></td><td><p>Offline</p></td><td><p>The data of the file is not immediately available. Indicates that the file data has been physically moved to offline storage.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Returns**

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Attributes set</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
$Result = SetFileAttr(@LDRIVE + "\Kix32.exe", 32)
```

---

### SETFOCUS

**Action**

Sets the input focus to the application specified. This function is very useful in combination with the SendKeys function.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETFOCUS ("Title")
```

**Parameters**

- **Title**: String specifying the title in the title bar of the application window you want to activate. In determining which application to activate, *title* is compared to the title string of each running application. If there is no exact match, any application whose title string begins with *title* is activated. If there is more than one instance of the application named by *title*, one instance is arbitrarily activated.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Focus set to specified application.</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
If SetFocus ("Untitled - Notepad") = 0
   ? "Focus set to Notepad...."
Endif
```

---

### SETOPTION

**Action**

SetOption can be used to configure certain options of the KiXtart script engine.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETOPTION("option", "value")
```

**Parameters**

- **Option**: Option to set.

- **Value**: Value for the option.

  - The options and possible values are described in the following table:

<table><thead><tr><th><p>ASCII</p></th><th><p>ON, OFF</p></th><th><p>Default console output is in Unicode. Setting the ASCII option to ON changes the output to ASCII, so you can output extended characters, such as line characters.</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CaseSensitivity</p></td><td><p>ON, OFF</p></td><td><p>By default, all string comparisons are case-insensitive. Setting the CaseSensitivity option to ON configures KiXtart to make string comparisons case-sensitive.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>DisableDebugging</p></td><td><p>ON</p></td><td><p>Disables debugging (effectively disables the DEBUG ON command). Note that debugging can not be re-enabled.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Explicit</p></td><td><p>ON, OFF</p></td><td><p>When you enable the Explicit option, you must explicitly declare all variables using the Dim, Global, or ReDim statements. If you attempt to use an undeclared variable name, an error occurs.</p><p>Use the Explicit option to avoid incorrectly typing the name of an existing variable.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>HideCursor</p></td><td><p>ON,OFF</p></td><td><p>Hides or shows the console cursor.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>NoMacrosInStrings</p></td><td><p>ON,OFF</p></td><td><p>Determines resolution of macros inside strings. If this option is enabled, any ‘@’ character in a string will be left as-is. The default is OFF.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>NoVarsInStrings</p></td><td><p>ON,OFF</p></td><td><p>Determines resolution of variables inside strings. If this option is enabled, any ‘$’ character in a string will be left as-is. The default is OFF.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Wow64FileRedirection</p></td><td><p>ON,OFF</p></td><td><p>Enables/disables file redirection on 64-bit editions of Windows. See <a href="http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/aa384187(VS.85).aspx">this MSDN article</a> for details of this feature. The default is ON.</p><p>This option has no effect on 32-bit editions of Windows.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>WOW64AlternateRegView</p></td><td><p>ON,OFF</p></td><td><p>Enables access to the alternate view of the registry. See <a href="http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/aa384232(VS.85).aspx">this MSDN article</a> for details of this feature. The default is OFF. This option has no effect on 32-bit editions of Windows.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>WrapAtEOL</p></td><td><p>ON,OFF</p></td><td><p>Enables/disables wrapping of console output at the end of a line. The default is OFF.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

SetOption returns the previous state of the option.

**Example**

```vb
$previousstate = SetOption( "ASCII", "ON" )

? "═══════════"    ; output ASCII line characters

$previousstate = SetOption( "ASCII", $previousstate )
```

---

### SETSYSTEMSTATE

**Action**

Changes the (power)state of the computer.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETSYSTEMSTATE (mode, force)
```

**Parameters**

- **Mode**: Optional parameter specifying one of the following modes:

<table><thead><tr><th><p>Value</p></th><th><p>Action</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>0</strong></p></td><td><p>Lock system (supported on Windows 2000 or higher only).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>1</strong></p></td><td><p>Standby.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>2</strong></p></td><td><p>Hibernate.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>3</strong></p></td><td><p>Poweroff.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

- **Force**: Specifies whether applications with unsaved changes are forcibly closed. If *force* is not zero, applications are closed. If *force* is zero, a dialog box is displayed prompting the user to close the applications.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Action succeeded</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>System error code</p></td><td><p>Action failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
$RC = SetSystemState( 1 , 1 )  ; Force system to StandBy mode
```

---

### SETTITLE

**Action**

Sets the title of the current console.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETTITLE("title")
```

**Parameters**

- **Title**: String that will be used as the new title for the current console.

**Remarks**

The title is only active while KiXtart runs. As soon as KiXtart exits, the original title of the console will be restored.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Title set</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
If SetTitle ("KiXtart Logon") = 0
   ? "Set the new title...."
Endif
```

---

### SETWALLPAPER

**Action**

Sets the current wallpaper.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETWALLPAPER("wallpaper name", mode)
```

**Parameters**

- **Wallpaper name**: String that specifies the name of the bitmap to set as the default wallpaper.

- **Mode**: Optional parameter specifying one of the following modes:

<table><thead><tr><th><p>Value</p></th><th><p>Action</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>0</strong></p></td><td><p>Only change the wallpaper for the duration of the current logon session.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>1</strong></p></td><td><p>Changes the wallpaper in the user profile.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Remarks**

The file specified must be a valid BMP file.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Wallpaper set</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
If SetWallpaper ("kixtart.bmp") = 0
   ? "Set current wallpaper to KiXtart.bmp...."
Endif
```

---

### SHOWPROGRAMGROUP

**Action**

Instructs Program Manager to minimize, maximize, or restore the window of an existing program group.

**Syntax**

```vb
SHOWPROGRAMGROUP ("group name", show command, common group flag)
```

**Parameters**

- **Group name**: Identifies the group window to minimize, maximize, or restore.

- **Show command**: Specifies the action Program Manager is to perform on the group window. This parameter is an integer and it must have one of the following values.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>Value</p></th><th><p>Action</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>1</strong></p></td><td><p>Activates and displays the group window. If the window is minimized or maximized, Windows restores it to its original size and position.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>2</strong></p></td><td><p>Activates the group window and displays it as an icon.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>3</strong></p></td><td><p>Activates the group window and displays it as a maximized window.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>4</strong></p></td><td><p>Displays the group window in its most recent size and position. The active window remains active.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>5</strong></p></td><td><p>Activates the group window and displays it in its current size and position.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>6</strong></p></td><td><p>Minimizes the group window.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>7</strong></p></td><td><p>Displays the group window as an icon. The active window remains active.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>8</strong></p></td><td><p>Displays the group window in its current state. The active window remains active.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

- **Common group flag**: Optional numeric parameter. *Common group flag* can have the following values:

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Acts upon a personal group.</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>1</p></td><td><p>Acts upon a common group. To manipulate a common group, the user must have administrative privileges, or the function fails.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Program group maximized, minimized, or restored</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
If ShowProgramGroup("NewGroup", 6, 0) = 0
   ? "NewGroup has been minimized...."
Endif
```

---

### SHUTDOWN

**Action**

Shuts down or reboots a computer.

**Syntax**

```vb
SHUTDOWN ("computer", "message", wait, force, options)
```

**Parameters**

- **Computer**: The name of the computer that is to be shut down or rebooted. An empty string("") indicates the local computer.

- **Message**: String that specifies a message to display in the **Shutdown** dialog box.

- **Wait**: Optional parameter specifying the time in seconds that the dialog box is displayed. While the dialog box is displayed, system shutdown can be stopped by using the Win32 **AbortSystemShutdown** function.

  - If *wait* is not zero, **SHUTDOWN** displays a dialog box on the specified computer. The dialog box, which displays the name of the user who called the function and the message specified by *message*, prompts the user to log off. The system beeps when the dialog box is created.

  - The dialog box remains on top of other windows and can be moved but not closed. A timer counts down the time remaining before a forced shutdown. If the user logs off, the system shuts down immediately. Otherwise, the computer is shut down when the timer expires.

  - If *wait *is zero, the computer shuts down without displaying the dialog box, and the shutdown cannot be stopped by **AbortSystemShutdown**.

- **Force**: Specifies whether applications with unsaved changes are forcibly closed. If *force* is not zero, applications are closed. If *force* is zero, a dialog box is displayed prompting the user to close the applications.

- **Options**: Optional parameter specifying one of the following options.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>Value</p></th><th><p>Action</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>1</strong></p></td><td><p>Reboot computer after shutdown.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>2</strong></p></td><td><p>Poweroff the system after shutdown (NB: this option only works for the local system).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Computer shut down</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>System error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
$RC = Shutdown("", "System is being rebooted to enable new settings.", 60, 0, 1)
```

---

### SIDTONAME

**Action**

Translates a Security Identifier (SID) into a name.

**Syntax**

```vb
SIDTONAME ("sid")
```

**Parameter**

- **SID**: String representation of SID to translate.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Name corresponding to SID.</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
? SidToName( "S-1-1-0")    ; displays ‘Everyone’

If InGroup( SidToName( "S-1-5-32-544" ) )
   ? "Must mean current user is a member of local Administrators"
Endif
```

---

### SPLIT

**Action**

Returns a zero-based, one-dimensional array containing a specified number of substrings.

**Syntax**

```vb
SPLIT ("string", "delimiter", count)
```

**Parameter**

- **String**: Required. String expression containing substrings and delimiters. If expression is a zero-length string, Split returns an empty array, that is, an array with no elements and no data.

- **Delimiter**: Optional. String character(s) used to identify substring limits. If omitted, the space character (" ") is assumed to be the delimiter. If delimiter is a zero-length string, a single-element array containing the entire expression string is returned.

- **Count**: Optional. Number of substrings to be returned; -1 indicates that all substrings are returned.

**Returns**

An array containing the substrings found in the input string.

**Example**

```vb
$Myarray = Split("aaa~~bbb~~ccc", "~~")

For Each $Element In $MyArray
   ? $Element        ; will display "aaa", "bbb" and "ccc"
Next
```

---

### SRND

**Action**

The SRND function sets the starting point for generating a series of pseudorandom integers. To reinitialize the generator, use 1 as the seed argument. Any other value for seed sets the generator to a random starting point. RND retrieves the pseudorandom numbers that are generated. Calling RND before any call to SRND generates the same sequence as calling SRND with seed passed as 1.

**Syntax**

```vb
SRND ( seed )
```

**Parameter**

- **Seed**: Numeric value to seed the generator with.

**Returns**

Nothing.

**Example**

```vb
SRND( @MSECS )
```

---

### SUBSTR

**Action**

Returns part of a string.

**Syntax**

**SUBSTR** ("*string*", *start*, *length*)

**Parameters**

- **String**: The string from which to extract a substring.

- **Start**: Numeric value representing the offset in the string where the substring begins.

- **Length**: Optional numeric value representing the length of the substring. If omitted or if there are fewer than Length characters in the text (including the character at start), all characters from the start position to the end of the string are returned.

**Returns**

The substring indicated by *start* and *length*.

**Example**

```vb
$x = SUBSTR(@USERID, LEN(@USERID) - 1, 2)   ; get the last 2 chars of the userid
```

---

### TRIM

**Action**

Strips leading and trailing spaces from an input string and returns the result.

**Syntax**

```vb
TRIM ("string")
```

**Parameter**

- **String**: The string from which to strip spaces.

**Returns**

The input string without leading and trailing spaces.

**Example**

```vb
$x = TRIM(SUBSTR(@IPADDRESS0, 1, 3))
```

---

### UBOUND

**Action**

Returns the largest available subscript for one of the dimensions of an array.

**Syntax**

```vb
UBOUND (array, dimension)
```

**Parameter**

- **Array**: The array you want to know the upper boundary of.

- **Dimension**: Optional parameter indicating the dimension of the array you want to know the upper boundary of. The default is 1.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>-1</p></th><th><p>Array dimension has zero elements.</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>&gt;= 0</p></td><td><p>Largest available subscript for the indicated dimension of the array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
$x = UBOUND($MyArray)
```

---

### UCASE

**Action**

Returns a string in uppercase.

**Syntax**

```vb
UCASE ("string")
```

**Parameter**

- **String**: The string you want to change to uppercase.

**Returns**

The input string in uppercase.

**Example**

```vb
$x = UCASE(@USERID)
```

---

### UNLOADHIVE

**Action**

Unloads the specified key and subkeys from the registry.

**Syntax**

```vb
UNLOADHIVE ("key")
```

**Parameters**

- **Key**: The key you want to unload. This key must have been created using LoadHive.

**Remarks**

On Windows NT, using **UNLOADHIVE** requires Backup and Restore privileges.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Key loaded</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

$ReturnCode = UnLoadHive( "HKEY\_USERS\\Fiets" )

If $ReturnCode = 0

? "Hive unloaded...."

```vb
Endif
```

---

### VAL

**Action**

Returns the numeric value of a string.

**Syntax**

```vb
VAL ("string")
```

**Parameter**

- **String**: The string whose numeric value you want to discover. By default, Val expects the string to be in decimal format. To determine the numeric value of a hexadecimal string, start the string with an ampersand ‘&amp;’.

**Returns**

The numeric value of the input string.

**Examples**

```vb
$x = VAL(SUBSTR(@IPADDRESS0, 1, 3))
$x = VAL("&A34")
```

---

### VARTYPE

**Action**

Returns an integer value indicating the subtype of a variable.

**Syntax**

```vb
VARTYPE($variable)
```

**Parameters**

- **Variable**: Any type of variable.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Empty</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>1</p></td><td><p>Null (no valid data)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>2</p></td><td><p>Integer</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>3</p></td><td><p>Long integer</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>4</p></td><td><p>Single-precision floating-point number</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>5</p></td><td><p>Double-precision floating-point number</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>6</p></td><td><p>Currency</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>7</p></td><td><p>Date</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>8</p></td><td><p>String</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>9</p></td><td><p>Object handle</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>10</p></td><td><p>Error</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>11</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>12</p></td><td><p>Variant (used only with arrays of Variants)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>13</p></td><td><p>A data-access object</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>17</p></td><td><p>Byte</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>8192</p></td><td><p>Array (can only occur in combination with one of the other values)</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
$MyVar = "AnyData"
? VarType( $MyVar )            ; will display "8" (String)

$MyArray = "a","b","c"
? VarType( $MyArray )        ; will display "8204" (Array of variants)
? VarType( $MyArray[0] )        ; will display "8" (String)
```

---

### VARTYPENAME

**Action**

Returns a string that provides type information about a variable.

**Syntax**

**VARTYPENAME** ($variable)

**Parameters**

- **Variable**: Any type of variable.

**Returns**

<table><thead><tr><th><p>Byte</p></th><th><p>Byte value</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Char</p></td><td><p>Single character value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Integer</p></td><td><p>Integer value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Long</p></td><td><p>Long integer value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Single</p></td><td><p>Single-precision floating-point value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Double</p></td><td><p>Double-precision floating-point value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Currency</p></td><td><p>Currency value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Decimal</p></td><td><p>Decimal value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Date</p></td><td><p>Date or time value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Character string value</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><p>Boolean value; True or False</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Empty</p></td><td><p>Unitialized</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Null</p></td><td><p>No valid data</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Object</p></td><td><p>Generic object</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Unknown</p></td><td><p>Unknown object type</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Nothing</p></td><td><p>Object variable that doesn&#x27;t yet refer to an object instance</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Error</p></td><td><p>Error</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>[ ]</p></td><td><p>Array (can only occur in combination with one of the other values)</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
? VarTypeName("KiXtart")    ; Displays "String".
? VarTypeName(4)            ; Displays "Long".
? VarTypeName(37.50)        ; Displays "Double".
? VarTypeName($ArrayVar)     ; Displays "Variant[]".
```

---

### WRITELINE

**Action**

Appends a line to the end of a file. If WriteLine encounters an error, @ERROR is set to the relevant errorcode.

**Syntax**

```vb
WRITELINE (file handle, "linetowrite")
```

**Parameter**

- **File handle**: A numeric expression indicating the file handle of the file to append to. Possible values range from 1 to 10.

- **LineToWrite**: The string you want to write to the file.

**Remarks**

WriteLine does not automatically append a &lt;Carriage Return&gt;, so if you want to write a &lt;Carriage Return&gt;, you should add it to the string (as in : $LineToWrite + @CRLF).

**Returns**

<table><thead><tr><th><p>-4</p></th><th><p>File not open for writing</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>-3</p></td><td><p>File handle not open</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>-2</p></td><td><p>Invalid file handle specified</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>-1</p></td><td><p>End of file</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>0</p></td><td><p>Line written successfully</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
IF Open( 3 , "C:\TEMP\LOG.TXT" , 5 )  = 0
   $x = WriteLine( 3 , "KiXtart started at " +  @TIME + @CRLF
ELSE
   BEEP
   ? "failed to open file, error code : [" + @ERROR + "]"
ENDIF

```

---

### WRITEPROFILESTRING

**Action**

Copies a string to an initialization file.

**Syntax**

```vb
WRITEPROFILESTRING ("file name", "section", "key", "string")
```

**Parameters**

- **File name**: String identifying the initialization file. If this parameter does not include a full path, Windows searches for the file in the Windows directory.

- **Section**: String containing the name of the section of the initialization file where *string* is copied. If the section does not exist, it is created. The section name is not case-sensitive, and can contain any combination of uppercase and lowercase letters.

- **Key**: String containing the name of the key to associate with *string*. If the key does not exist in the specified section, it is created. If this parameter is empty, the entire section, including all entries within the section, is deleted.

- **String**: String to write to the file. If this parameter is empty, the key identified by *key* is deleted.

**Remarks**

This function is provided for compatibility with 16-bit Windows-based applications. Win32-based applications store initialization information in the registry.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Profile string written</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

---

### WRITEVALUE

**Action**

Creates a new key, adds another value-name to an existing key (and assigns it a value), or changes the value of an existing value-name.

**Syntax**

**WRITEVALUE** ("*subkey*", "*entry*", "*expression*", "*data type*")

**Parameters**

- **Subkey**: Identifies the subkey where you want to write a value entry.

- **Entry**: The name of the entry. To write to the default entry of a key, specify an empty string as the entry name ("").

- **Expression**: The data to store as the value of the entry.

**REG\_MULTI\_SZ** (multi-string) variables are returned with the pipe symbol ( | ) used as the separator between strings. If a string contains a pipe symbol character, it is represented by two pipe symbol characters ( || ).

- **Data type**: Identifies the data type of the entry.

  - The following data types are supported:

    **REG\_NONE**

    **REG\_SZ**

    **REG\_EXPAND\_SZ**

    **REG\_BINARY**

    **REG\_DWORD**

    **REG\_DWORD\_LITTLE\_ENDIAN**

    **REG\_DWORD\_BIG\_ENDIAN**

    **REG\_LINK**

    **REG\_MULTI\_SZ**

    **REG\_RESOURCE\_LIST**

    **REG\_FULL\_RESOURCE\_DESCRIPTOR**

**Returns**

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Value entry written</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Error code</p></td><td><p>Function failed</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

```vb
WriteValue("EZReg\Test", "A MultiString variable", "Line 1|Line 2|Line 3 with a || in it|", "REG_MULTI_SZ")
If @ERROR = 0
   ? "Value written to the registry"
Endif
```

# KiXtart Macro Reference

Macros can be used anywhere an expression is expected. Supported macros are defined in the following table.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>Macro</p></th><th><p>Definition</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>@ADDRESS</p></td><td><p>Address of the network adapter</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@BUILD</p></td><td><p>Build number of the operating system</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@COLOR</p></td><td><p>Current console colour setting</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@COMMENT</p></td><td><p>User comment</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@CPU</p></td><td><p>Name of the CPU (e.g.: &quot;Intel Pentium III&quot;).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@CRLF</p></td><td><p>Carriage-return + Line-feed</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@CSD</p></td><td><p>CSD information (e.g.: &quot;Service Pack 1&quot;)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@CURDIR</p></td><td><p>Current directory</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@DATE</p></td><td><p>Date (in the format YYYY/MM/DD)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@DAY</p></td><td><p>Day of the week (Monday, Tuesday, and so on)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@DOMAIN</p></td><td><p>Domain or workgroup the computer belongs to</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@DOS</p></td><td><p>Windows Version</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@ERROR</p></td><td><p>Return code of the most recent command or function. A return code of 0 means the command or function was successful. Any other value indicates an error.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@FULLNAME</p></td><td><p>Full name of current user</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@HOMEDIR</p></td><td><p>Short name of the directory part of home directory</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@HOMEDRIVE*</p></td><td><p>Drive letter of drive containing home directory</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@HOMESHR</p></td><td><p>Server and share name part of home directory</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@HOSTNAME</p></td><td><p>Fully qualified TCP/IP host name (including TCP/IP domain name)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@INUDF</p></td><td><p>Name of the current user-defined function.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@INWIN</p></td><td><p>Operating system: 1 = Windows NT; 2 = Windows 9x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@IPADDRESS<em>x</em></p></td><td><p>TCP/IP address (possible values for <em>x</em> are 0 - 3). <br /><strong>Note   </strong>Addresses are padded so that the resulting string always consists of four sets of three characters separated by periods. For example, if your IP address is 123.45.6.7, @IPADDRESS0 is 123. 45. 6. 7.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@KIX</p></td><td><p>KiXtart product name and version</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@LANROOT</p></td><td><p>Directory where network software resides (usually <em>Systemroot</em>\System32)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@LDOMAIN*</p></td><td><p>Logon domain</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@LDRIVE</p></td><td><p>Drive that is redirected to \\logonserver\NETLOGON</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@LM</p></td><td><p>Version of network software</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@LOGONMODE</p></td><td><p>If 1, indicates that KiXtart assumes to be running during the logon sequence</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@LONGHOMEDIR</p></td><td><p>Long name of the directory part of home directory</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@LSERVER</p></td><td><p>Logon server</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@MAXPWAGE</p></td><td><p>Maximum password age</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@MDAYNO</p></td><td><p>Day of the month (1-31)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@MHZ</p></td><td><p>Approximation of the CPU speed.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@MONTHNO</p></td><td><p>Months since January (1-12)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@MONTH</p></td><td><p>Name of the month</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@MSECS</p></td><td><p>Milliseconds part of the current time</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@ONWOW64</p></td><td><p>If this macro returns 1, KiXtart is running in the <a href="http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/en-us/win64/win64/running_32_bit_applications.asp">WOW64</a> environment on a Windows x64 system.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@PID</p></td><td><p>Process ID of the KiXtart process</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@PRIMARYGROUP*</p></td><td><p>Current user&#x27;s primary group</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@PRIV</p></td><td><p>User&#x27;s privilege level (GUEST, USER, ADMIN)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@PRODUCTSUITE</p></td><td><p>OS suite. Combination of any of the following values:</p><p>1    - &quot;Small Business&quot;</p><p>2    - &quot;Enterprise&quot;</p><p>4    - &quot;BackOffice&quot;</p><p>8    - &quot;CommunicationServer&quot;</p><p>16   - &quot;Terminal Server&quot;</p><p>32   - &quot;Small Business (Restricted)&quot;</p><p>64   - &quot;EmbeddedNT&quot;</p><p>128  - &quot;DataCenter&quot;</p><p>256  - &quot;Single user Terminal Server&quot;</p><p>512  - &quot;Home Edition&quot;</p><p>1024 - &quot;Blade Server&quot;</p><p>2048 - &quot;Embedded (Restricted)&quot;</p><p>4096 - &quot;Security Appliance&quot;</p><p>8192 - &quot;Storage Server&quot;</p><p>16384- &quot;Compute Cluster Server&quot;</p><p>32768- &quot;Home Server&quot;</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@PRODUCTTYPE</p></td><td><p>OS type. Possible values range from Windows 2000, Windows XP to Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@PROGRAMFILESX86</p></td><td><p>X86 Program Files directory</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@PWAGE</p></td><td><p>Password age</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@RAS</p></td><td><p>Number of active Remote Access Service (RAS) connections</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@RESULT</p></td><td><p>Returns command specific information (e.g.: the drive letter of an automatic redirection command)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@RELEASEID</p></td><td><p>Windows release ID (eg: 1909, 2003, etc)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@RELEASENAME</p></td><td><p>Windows release name (eg: 21H2)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@RSERVER*</p></td><td><p>KXRPC server used for the current session</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@SCRIPTDIR</p></td><td><p>Directory of current script</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@SCRIPTEXE</p></td><td><p>Name of KiXtart executable (&quot;KIX32.EXE&quot;, &quot;WKIX32.EXE&quot;)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@SCRIPTNAME</p></td><td><p>Name of current script</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@SERROR</p></td><td><p>Error text corresponding with @ERROR</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@SID*</p></td><td><p>Current user&#x27;s Windows  Security Identifier (SID)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@SITE**</p></td><td><p>Name of the site in which the system resides</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@STARTDIR</p></td><td><p>Directory from which KiXtart was started</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@SYSLANG</p></td><td><p>Full English name of the language of the operating system specified in the format defined by  ISO Standard 639. (example : &quot;0413Dutch (Standard)&quot;).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@TICKS</p></td><td><p>Returns the number of milliseconds that have elapsed since the system was started.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@TIME</p></td><td><p>Current time (in the format HH:MM:SS)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@TSSESSION</p></td><td><p>If this macro returns 1, KiXtart is running in a Terminal Server session.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@USERID</p></td><td><p>Current user&#x27;s Windows  user ID</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@USERLANG</p></td><td><p>Full English name of the language selected by the current user specified in the format defined by  ISO Standard 639. (example : &quot;0413Dutch (Standard)&quot;).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@WDAYNO</p></td><td><p>Days since Sunday (1 – 7)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@WKSTA</p></td><td><p>Computer name</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@WUSERID</p></td><td><p>Current user&#x27;s Windows user ID</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@YDAYNO</p></td><td><p>Days since January 1 (1 – 365)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@YEAR</p></td><td><p>Current year</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

The following examples show the correct use of KiXtart macros:

@LM        "2.10"

@DATE        "1997/10/03"

DISPLAY @USERID + ".TXT"        displays the file "RUUDV.TXT"

CD "\\DATA\\" + @DOMAIN        changes the current directory to "\\DATA\\your-domain"<br>

# KiXtart COM Automation Implementation

The following paragraphs describe the KiXtart COM automation implementation.

# Installing the KiXtart COM implementation

To install the KiXtart COM Automation implementation, copy KIXTART.DLL to a location on your system and run the following command (using an account with administrative privileges):

REGSVR32 KIXTART.DLL

Note the COM implementation of KiXtart, provided in KiXtart.dll is a standalone product. It is not required by Kix32 or vice versa.

## Using the KiXtart COM implementation

Once installed, the KiXtart COM interface can be used from within any 32-bit Windows application that supports COM automation (such as Microsoft Excel, Visual Basic Script, etc).

The KiXtart COM component is called "KiXtart.Application" and can be instantiated using the CreateObject call:

CreateObject("KiXtart.Application")

## KiXtart COM methods

The KiXtart COM interface supports the following methods:

---

### RUNSCRIPT

**Action**

Runs a KiXtart script.

**Syntax**

```vb
RUNSCRIPT ("script name", "script password", asynchronous)
```

**Parameters**

- **Script name**: String identifying the script to run.

- **Script password**: Optional string containing the password with which the script was encrypted.

- **Asynchronous**: Optional value specifying whether or not the script will run synchronous (the default) or asynchronous. This parameter can have the following values:

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Run synchronous (default).</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>1</p></td><td><p>Run asynchronous.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Remarks**

Only 1 script can be run at the same time.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Script started</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>&lt;&gt;0</p></td><td><p>Failed to start script</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

Set oMy = CreateObject("KiXtart.Application")

If err.number = 0 Then

x = oMy.RunScript("demo.kix")

…

…

End If

---

### TERMINATESCRIPT

**Action**

TerminateScript can be used to stop execution of a currently running script..

**Syntax**

```vb
TERMINATESCRIPT (force)
```

**Parameters**

- **Force**: Optional value specifying whether or not the script should be stopped forecefully. This parameter can have the following values:

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Terminate script (default).</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>1</p></td><td><p>Terminate script forcefully.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Remarks**

Forcefully terminating a script can have undesirable side-effects (such as memory not being released) and should only be used as a last resort to stop a hanging script.

<table><thead><tr><th><p>0</p></th><th><p>Script terminated</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>&lt;&gt;0</p></td><td><p>Failed to terminate script</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

Set oMy = CreateObject("KiXtart.Application")

If err.number = 0 Then

x = oMy.RunScript( "test.kix" ,  , true )  ' run async

stat = oMy.ScriptStatus   ' wait for script to end

waited = 0

Do While stat = 259 And waited &lt; 20

waited = waited + 1

wscript.sleep(1000)

stat = oMy.ScriptStatus

Loop

If waited = 20 Then

' this has taken too long, terminate script

x = oMy.TerminateScript()

End If

End If

---

### GETVAR

**Action**

GetVar can be used to retrieve the value of global variables after a script has run.

**Syntax**

```vb
GETVAR (“variable name”)
```

**Parameters**

- **Variable name**: String value identifying the variable to return the value of. Note that the names of all KiXtart variables start with a “$”, but that you can call GetVar with or without the “$”.

**Returns**

  - Value of the requested variable.

**Example**

Set oMy = CreateObject("KiXtart.Application")

If err.number = 0 Then

x = oMy.RunScript("demo.kix")

x = oMy.GetVar( "MyVar" )

…

…

End If

---

### SETVAR

**Action**

SetVar can be used to set the value of global variables before or after running a script.

**Syntax**

```vb
SETVAR (“variable name”, new value)
```

**Parameters**

- **Variable name**: String value identifying the variable to set the value of. If the variable does not yet exists, SetVar will create it. Note that the names of all KiXtart variables start with a “$”, but that you can call SetVar with or without the “$”.

- **New value**: Variant value containing the value to set.

**Returns**

<table><thead><tr><th><p>FALSE</p></th><th><p>Failed to set variable.</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>TRUE</p></td><td><p>Successfully assigned the value to the variable</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

Set oMy = CreateObject("KiXtart.Application")

If err.number = 0 Then

x = oMy.SetVar( "MyVar", 12345 )

x = oMy.SetVar( "$MyVar", 12345 )  ‘ same as above…

x = oMy.RunScript("demo.kix")

…

…

## KiXtart COM properties

The KiXtart COM interface supports the following properties:

---

### SCRIPTSTATUS

**Description**

ScriptStatus can be used to retrieve the exitcode of a script. If the script is still running, ScriptStatus will be 259.

**Returns**

<table><thead><tr><th><p>259</p></th><th><p>Script still running</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>&lt;&gt;259</p></td><td><p>Script exit code</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

**Example**

Set oMy = CreateObject("KiXtart.Application")

If err.number = 0 Then

x = oMy.RunScript( "test.kix",, true )  ' run async

stat = oMy.ScriptStatus   ' wait for script to end

waited = 0

Do While stat = 259 And waited &lt; 20

waited = waited + 1

wscript.sleep(1000)

stat = oMy.ScriptStatus

Loop

If waited = 20 Then

' this has taken too long, terminate script

x = oMy.TerminateScript()

End If

End If

---

### &lt;KIXTART MACROS&gt;

**Description**

The KiXtart COM interface provides access to all KiXtart macros.

**Example**

Set oMy = CreateObject("KiXtart.Application")

If err.number = 0 Then

‘ display some of KiXtart’s macros

Wscript.Echo oMy.CPU

Wscript.Echo oMy.USERID

Wscript.Echo oMy.LDOMAIN

…

…

End If

<br>

# APPENDIX A: Error handling

To find out if a KiXtart command or function is successful, always check the @ERROR and @SERROR macros. Note that most functions also return the error code.

If @ERROR is zero, the previous command or function was successful. If @ERROR is non-zero, the value corresponds to the error code returned by the most recently executed Win32 API.

To find out what a specific error code means, please consult the list of Win32 error codes on the Microsoft Developer Network ([https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms681381(v=vs.85).aspx](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms681381(v=vs.85).aspx)).<br>

# Where to find more information

If you are interested in discussing tips and tricks on KiXtart usage and/or in sharing sample scripts, please consider joining one of the international communities of KiXtart users. To do so, connect to: [http://kixtart.org](http://kixtart.org/)

If you have feedback or questions regarding KiXtart, feel free to contact [ruudv@microsoft.com](mailto:ruudv@microsoft.com).

To find the latest versions of KiXtart, and more sample scripts and tips and tricks on KiXtart, please visit

[http://kixtart.org](http://kixtart.org/).

If you want to read more about KiXtart, please consider these excellent books:

[http://www.amazon.com/Finish-Scripting-Kixtart-Guides-Agility/dp/1932577092](http://www.amazon.com/Finish-Scripting-Kixtart-Guides-Agility/dp/1932577092)

[http://www.amazon.com/Windows-Admin-Scripting-Little-Black/dp/1932111158](http://www.amazon.com/Windows-Admin-Scripting-Little-Black/dp/1932111158).

<br>

# Acknowledgements

KiXtart is the result of feedback, suggestions and ideas from people all over the world and from all types of organizations. Their passionate discussions, frantic testing and even scripting competitions have greatly helped to produce the truly exciting end result that is now called KiXtart 2010.

I would like to express my sincerest thanks to all of you, and by all means: Keep Scripting!

Very special thanks go out to all the enthusiastic die-hards who over the years supported KiXtart (and kept me alive) by hosting and/or actively participating in KiXtart web-sites, forums, mail-lists and bulletin boards. The group of people involved is far too large to list in full, but some names absolutely require mentioning: Steve Wilson, Steve Ognibene, Larry Duncan, Brian Styles, Jim Kay,  Jooel Nieminen, Howard Bullock, Bob Kelly, Shawn Tassie, Kevin Cowans, Kent Dyer, Les Ligetfalvy, Erik Kærholm, Jochen Polster, Chris Matheson, Bryan Steele, Brad Schunk, Ben Burnett, Rob Butler and of course Henri Wiering of [http://kixtart.org](http://kixtart.org/): thanks guys, you really make a difference!

Once again, my sincerest thanks to all of you, and I hope to meet you again in the next release…!<br>

# About KiXtart

KiXtart is a spare time project of Ruud van Velsen of Microsoft Netherlands.

KiXtart was developed on Windows 11 using Microsoft Visual Studio 2019 and the Windows Software Development Kit.

The SPK format used by the **PLAY** command was originally designed by Gordon E. Peterson II. The SPK files were translated from BASIC and Assembler programs gathered from various public domain sources.
